quiz.question: The CUBE platform stores its session token in a cookie named `jwtToken`. What is the token's actual content and why is the name misleading?
options: A signed JWT payload containing userId, portalId, and expiry — the name is accurate
A UUID v4 session ID that maps to server-side Redis state — the name is misleading because it is not a JWT
A base64-encoded user object stored in localStorage for fast access
An RS256-signed token generated by the Jitsi integration and reused for API auth
quiz.explanation: The cookie holds a UUID v4 session ID. The actual user data lives in Redis at `userSession:{userId}:{portalId}`. It is session-based auth, not JWT. True JWT (RS256-signed) is only used for Jitsi collaboration tokens. The httpOnly flag on the cookie prevents JavaScript from reading it, protecting against XSS.

quiz.question: Why does the Redux caching layer for `UserNameTagComponent` store profile images as Base64 data URLs rather than storing the image URL string?
options: Base64 compresses images more efficiently than serving them over HTTP
Storing a URL still triggers a browser image request on every render, including a 304 round-trip on cache hits; a Base64 data URL embedded in the src attribute requires zero network calls
The image server does not support HTTP caching headers, making URL storage unreliable
Redux cannot serialize URL strings, only binary-safe Base64 values
quiz.explanation: Even a 304 Not Modified response costs a 50–200 ms round-trip. Hard refresh clears the disk cache. Missing Cache-Control headers cause full re-downloads. By fetching the image blob once, converting via FileReader.readAsDataURL(), and storing the Base64 string in Redux, the component's src attribute IS the image — no HTTP request is ever made again, not even a conditional one.

quiz.question: What is the algorithmic complexity of the optimized tree filter, and what data structure makes the improvement possible?
options: O(n log n) — achieved by sorting nodes by depth before traversal
O(n) — achieved by using a Set of already-included IDs to short-circuit parent chain walks
O(1) amortized — achieved by memoizing the parent chain for each node at load time
O(n²) — the naive approach is unavoidable for tree-structured data
quiz.explanation: The naive approach walks the parentId chain for every matching node independently, visiting the same ancestors repeatedly — O(n × depth). The optimized version builds a Set of included IDs and walks each parent chain only until it hits an ID already in the set. Because the set grows monotonically, each unique ancestor is visited at most once across the entire traversal, giving O(n) total.

quiz.question: Why does the platform use Redis for session validation rather than MongoDB, and what is the approximate latency difference?
options: Redis supports complex joins; MongoDB does not support session lookups
Redis reads from RAM in under 1 ms; MongoDB reads from disk at 5–50 ms — every single API request validates the session, making this difference critical at scale
MongoDB does not support TTL-based expiry, which is needed for inactivity timeout
Redis is cheaper to operate than MongoDB at the session validation query volume
quiz.explanation: Session validation runs on every API request — thousands of reads per second. Redis serves from RAM in ~0.1 ms. MongoDB requires disk I/O at 5–50 ms per read. Using MongoDB for sessions would make every authenticated API call 50–500x slower. Redis also natively supports TTL for inactivity timeouts and the List data structure for max-simultaneous-login enforcement.

quiz.question: The Zoho Billing integration caches the OAuth access token in Redis with a 58-minute TTL, even though Zoho tokens expire at 60 minutes. Why the 2-minute buffer?
options: Redis TTL resolution is 2 minutes, so a 60-minute TTL would actually expire at 62 minutes
The 2-minute gap ensures the cached token is invalidated before Zoho invalidates it, preventing API calls from succeeding with a token that is about to expire mid-request
Zoho rate-limits token refresh calls to once every 58 minutes
The buffer accounts for clock skew between the CUBE server and Zoho's OAuth endpoint
quiz.explanation: If the Redis TTL matched Zoho's expiry exactly, a token retrieved from Redis at 59:59 could be expired by the time the Zoho API request arrives. The 58-minute TTL ensures the cache evicts the token 2 minutes early, forcing a fresh refresh before the window where the token might be rejected, preventing failed API calls.

quiz.question: SlickGrid renders 100k+ rows at 60fps. Which mechanism is primarily responsible for this performance, and what is the DOM state at any given scroll position?
options: React's virtual DOM diffing algorithm prevents expensive re-renders during scroll
DOM virtualization: only ~30–40 rows exist in the DOM at any time, recycled in-place as the user scrolls; the visible row index is calculated in O(1) as scrollTop / rowHeight
Web Workers offload row rendering to a background thread, keeping the main thread free
The grid pre-renders all 100k rows into a DocumentFragment and uses CSS transforms to position them
quiz.explanation: SlickGrid's DataView maintains a flat array of all rows but renders only the viewport-visible slice plus a buffer. Fixed row height makes the first visible index a simple division — O(1). As the user scrolls, existing DOM nodes are reused and their content replaced in-place. At no point do 100k DOM elements exist simultaneously.

flashcards.front: What is the three-state pattern used in the Redux `userTagData` slice, and why does it prevent race conditions?
flashcards.back: Each user ID entry has three states: `{ isLoading: false, isLoaded: false, data: null }` (empty), `{ isLoading: true, isLoaded: false, data: null }` (in-flight), and `{ isLoading: false, isLoaded: true, data: {...} }` (cached). The thunk checks `isLoading || isLoaded` at dispatch time using `getState()`. If either is true, it returns without making a network call. This means the first component to dispatch for a given user ID sets `isLoading: true`, and all subsequent components mounting in the same render cycle see that flag and skip the fetch — preventing N concurrent duplicate requests.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between session-based auth and JWT-based auth, and which does CUBE use?
flashcards.back: Session-based auth stores user state on the server (Redis) and gives the browser a random opaque ID (UUID). Every request requires the server to look up that ID. JWT-based auth encodes user state inside the token itself — the server only needs to verify the signature. CUBE uses session-based auth: the `jwtToken` cookie holds a UUID v4, not a signed payload. Sessions were chosen because they support instant revocation (delete the Redis key), max simultaneous login enforcement, and inactivity timeout — none of which are possible with stateless JWTs without a server-side blacklist.

flashcards.front: Why is the Razorpay integration described as indirect, and what does the actual payment flow look like?
flashcards.back: Razorpay is not integrated via SDK on the CUBE server. Zoho Billing acts as the orchestrator: CUBE's server calls Zoho's subscription API, Zoho manages the Razorpay checkout flow internally, and CUBE receives payment outcomes via Zoho webhooks (e.g., `payment_thankyou`, `subscription_activation`). The gateway selection is currency-driven: Razorpay for INR in development, Zoho Payments for INR in production, PayPal for USD. All webhook events are logged to a `zohoWebHooks` MongoDB collection before processing for auditability.

flashcards.front: What does `FileReader.readAsDataURL()` do in the image caching flow, and why is it used instead of storing the image URL?
flashcards.back: `FileReader.readAsDataURL()` reads a Blob (raw binary image data) and returns a Base64-encoded data URL string like `data:image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgo...`. When this string is set as the `src` of an `` element, the image data is embedded inline — the browser does not make any HTTP request. In contrast, storing the original URL means the browser issues a request on every render. Even a cached 304 response costs a round-trip. The data URL approach makes image rendering completely network-free after the first fetch.

flashcards.front: What Redis data structures does CUBE use for session management, and what does each one track?
flashcards.back: Three structures: (1) String keys `session:{sessionId}` → `userId` for the basic session-to-user lookup on every request. (2) List keys `userSessionsList:{userId}` — RPUSH on login, LRANGE to get all sessions, LINDEX to find the oldest, LREM on logout — for max simultaneous login enforcement and eviction of the oldest session. (3) Redis JSON keys `userSession:{userId}:{portalId}` — stores per-portal session objects with `lastAccessed` timestamps for inactivity timeout checking and session count via `json.OBJLEN`.

flashcards.front: How does the monorepo structure benefit the CUBE platform, and what are the three packages?
flashcards.back: The monorepo (`packages/web`, `packages/server`, `packages/storybook`) keeps TypeScript types shared between frontend and backend in sync without npm publish cycles. A change to a shared type is immediately visible to all three packages. Cross-cutting features (e.g., adding a new field to a user model) can be shipped in a single commit and PR that spans all three packages. The Storybook package imports components directly from the web package, so documentation always reflects the current implementation with no versioning drift.

flashcards.front: What is the Service Worker caching strategy used for load time improvement, and what trade-off does it introduce?
flashcards.back: Static assets (JS bundles, CSS, fonts) are pre-cached on Service Worker install. The fetch handler intercepts requests for these assets and serves from the Cache Storage API without consulting the network, reducing repeat-visit load time from 3 seconds to 800 ms. The trade-off: a stale Service Worker can serve outdated assets if the update lifecycle is mishandled. This is mitigated with `skipWaiting()` + `clientsClaim()` so new deployments activate the updated worker immediately across all open tabs rather than waiting for a full restart. API responses are explicitly excluded from the Service Worker cache.

flashcards.front: How does the SlickGrid Excel export handle grouped data with embedded images?
flashcards.back: The export walks the DataView's group structure recursively, flattening groups into rows with depth-level metadata. Group header rows become bold ExcelJS rows with outline levels (enabling collapse/expand in Excel). For image columns, all unique image URLs in the visible dataset are collected, then pre-fetched in parallel via `Promise.all()` as Buffers. Each buffer is added to the ExcelJS workbook's image store once, then referenced by cell position. This avoids the N+1 fetch problem: each unique image URL is downloaded exactly once regardless of how many rows reference it.

flashcards.front: What is the `portalRoleValidator` middleware pattern, and why is it implemented as a factory function?
flashcards.back: `portalRoleValidator(section, parameter)` is a higher-order function that returns an Express middleware. The returned middleware reads the portal settings object for the authenticated user and checks `settings[section][parameter]` (default parameter: `isVisible`). It is a factory because different routes need different permission checks: `portalRoleValidator('projects', 'canDelete')` vs `portalRoleValidator('portalUsers')`. The factory pattern allows the permission parameters to be declared statically at route registration time, keeping authorization logic co-located with the route definition and avoiding conditional branching inside a single generic middleware.

flashcards.front: What does the `isLoading` guard in the Redux thunk protect against, and what happens without it?
flashcards.back: The guard prevents duplicate in-flight requests for the same user ID. Without it: Component A dispatches for user `abc`, setting a fetch in motion but not yet receiving a response. Components B and C mount in the same render cycle, read `isLoaded: false` from the store (the response has not arrived), and also dispatch fetches. Three concurrent API calls fire for the same user ID. With the guard: Component A's dispatch immediately sets `isLoading: true`. Components B and C check `isLoading || isLoaded` — both are false for `isLoaded` but `isLoading` is now true — so they return without dispatching. Only one network call is ever made per user ID.

flashcards.front: What is the WebSocket collaboration architecture, what are its current scaling limits, and what is the path to fix them?
flashcards.back: Socket.IO rooms are keyed by `fileVersionId`. Users join rooms, and the server broadcasts 25+ event types (camera sync, selection, material changes, markup, cutting planes) via `socket.to(room).emit()`. Room state (`rooms[]`, `userinfo[]`) is stored in the Node.js process heap — so all users in a session must be connected to the same server. This limits horizontal scaling to a single instance. The fix: `@socket.io/redis-adapter` replaces in-process event routing with Redis pub/sub, allowing events from any server instance to reach sockets on any other instance. Room data would migrate to Redis JSON, mirroring the existing session management pattern.

flashcards.front: What is the `centralApiFunction` pattern for Zoho API calls, and what problem does it solve?
flashcards.back: `centralApiFunction({ method, endpoint, data, returnField })` is a single entry point for all Zoho Billing API calls. It calls `generateAccessToken()` first (which checks Redis for a cached token or performs the OAuth refresh grant), assembles the required headers (`X-com-zoho-subscriptions-organizationid`, `Authorization: Zoho-oauthtoken ...`), and executes the request via Axios. It solves the problem of scattered auth and header management: across dozens of Zoho API calls (customers, subscriptions, invoices, estimates), token refresh and error normalization happen in exactly one place. Changing the auth flow requires editing one function, not hunting through all callers.
